---
layout: post
title:  "1000 Years of Mars Oppositions (1600-2600)"
date:   2026-05-01 16:30:00 -0400
tags: astronomy observing
---

While there's a ~1-2 month window every ~780 days (~2 years, 1.5 months) when Mars viewing is best, the quality varies considerably around a roughly 20 year cycle.

I've found [some listings of Mars oppositions](https://spider.seds.org/spider/Mars/marsopps.html) to be useful for planning purposes and the like, but wanted a longer baseline to get a feel for some of those extremes. So I went to [JPL Horizons](https://ssd.jpl.nasa.gov/horizons/), and got ephemeris for Mars every 6 hours from 1800 CE to 2200 CE (and then expanded it to their full range of 1600 CE to 2600 CE).

([And all 469 as a CSV](/codeanddata/mars_1000_opp_final.csv). I'm not posting the full data unless someone asks because the CSV of that is 194 MB. I'm also trying to ignore the siren song of getting 1 hour intervals. Though in that case, I could probably just [calculate things locally with astropy](https://docs.astropy.org/en/latest/coordinates/solarsystem.html)…)

# Asides

There are very real patterns in right ascension and declination, which cause the most favorable opositions to be around august-september and in the southern hemisphere. The least favorable ones are in february-march and at modestly northern declinations. For a quick look with [TOPCAT](https://www.star.bristol.ac.uk/mbt/topcat/) graphs:

<img src="/images/MarsRASizeopp.png" alt="Mars opp size vs RA" title="Mars oppositions shown in RA vs angular size. Note the sinusoid." width="49%"/>
<img src="/images/MarsDecSizeopp.png" alt="Mars opp size vs dec" title="Mars oppositions shown in Dec vs angular size. There's an ellipse of sorts." width="49%"/>

<img src="/images/MarsRASize1000.png" alt="Mars 1000 year size vs RA" title="All 1000 years of Mars positions in RA vs angular size." width="49%"/>
<img src="/images/MarsDecSize1000.png" alt="Mars 1000 year size vs dec" title="All 1000 years of Mars positions in Dec vs angular size." width="49%"/>

Patterns like these seem pretty common in geocentric reference frames, really.

# Top/Bottom 6 Best/Worst Apparitions

|Date/Time|Right Ascension|Declination|Size (arcsec)|Distance (au)|
|-|-|-|-|-|
|2287-Aug-29 00:00|22 26 28.32|-16 54 46.2|25.15834|0.37225435|
|2571-Aug-30 06:00|22 14 02.84|-18 04 17.7|25.14966|0.37238281|
|2366-Sep-02 06:00|22 40 49.08|-15 23 50.3|25.14925|0.37238887|
|2003-Aug-27 12:00|22 38 35.28|-15 44 21.1|25.12695|0.37271929|
|2208-Aug-24 06:00|22 11 49.39|-18 22 38.5|25.12185|0.37279501|
|1924-Aug-23 00:00|22 24 05.38|-17 14 42.8|25.11843|0.37284572|

|Date/Time|Right Ascension|Declination|Size (arcsec)|Distance (au)|
|-|-|-|-|-|
|2548-Mar-03 12:00|10 33 05.08|+13 42 35.4|13.80808|0.67824793|
|2469-Feb-28 12:00|10 26 43.03|+14 23 00.1|13.80869|0.67821767|
|2390-Feb-26 06:00|10 20 33.17|+15 01 22.0|13.81251|0.67803021|
|2027-Feb-20 00:00|10 15 48.59|+15 33 17.3|13.81478|0.67791882|
|2106-Feb-23 06:00|10 22 01.50|+14 55 42.8|13.81551|0.67788282|
|2185-Feb-25 06:00|10 28 35.13|+14 15 15.1|13.81576|0.67787082|

Yes, that means that the upcoming 2027 aphelic apparition is the worst in at least 430 years and 4th most small/distant in the entire interval. The 2003 perhelic apparition being the best in recorded history (though there will be better ones in the future) is I feel already well known.
